elements.sent-2411-002: If the trader's valuation is lower than the bid price, or higher than the ask price, then a trade (sell or buy) occurs.

elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Regret minimization, online learning, market making, first-price auctions, dynamic pricing.
elements.sent-2411-009: Trading in financial markets is a crucial activity that helps keep the world's economy running, and several players, including hedge funds, prop trading firms, investment banks, central banks, and retail traders participate in it daily.
elements.sent-2411-010: While every actor has their own objective function (for example, a hedge fund wants to maximize profit whereas a central bank wants to keep inflation in check), at a fundamental level, trading can be viewed as a stochastic control problem where agents want the state to evolve so as to maximize their objective function.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this work we focus on market makers, i.e., traders whose job is to facilitate other trades to happen.
elements.sent-2411-012: One way to do so is by broadcasting, at all times, a price (bid) at which they are willing to buy, and a price (ask) at which they are willing to sell the asset being traded.
elements.sent-2411-013: This way when a buyer (seller) arrives, they do not have to wait for a seller (buyer) to be able to perform a transaction.
elements.sent-2411-014: A market where it is easy to make trades is called liquid, and liquidity is a desirable property for any kind of market.
elements.sent-2411-015: A market maker thus provides an essential service by increasing the liquidity of the market, and collects compensation for it by (among other ways) ensuring that the bid is always smaller than the ask, thus making a profit proportional to the difference between the bid and the ask.
elements.sent-2411-016: Market making is challenging, and a lot of thought goes into making it profitable, see~\cite{harris2003trading for an overview.}
elements.sent-2411-017: A major risk that a market maker has to deal with, called adverse selection, is the risk that your counterpart is an informed trader who knows something about the future direction of the price movement.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, an informed trader who knows that an asset is soon about to become cheaper will sell it to you thus forcing you to buy something whose price crashes.
elements.sent-2411-019: One way to mitigate this risk is to immediately offload your positions elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-020: This strategy can be profitable if, for example, you have access to two markets for the same asset where one market is less liquid than the other.
elements.sent-2411-021: You can be a market maker for the less liquid market while offloading your positions in the more liquid one.
elements.sent-2411-022: We call this strategy ``market making with instant clearing''.
elements.sent-2411-023: Perhaps closest to our work is the paper \citep{abernethy2013adaptive , where they compete against a class of $N$ constant-spread dynamic strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Although we also compete with constant-spread (static) strategies, their results are not directly comparable to ours for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, in their model the only unknown parameter is the market price, whose change from step to step is adversarial yet bounded by a known quantity.
elements.sent-2411-026: As the market price is revealed at the end of each step, their utility function at time $t$ is fully known at the end of step $t$.
elements.sent-2411-027: This implies that their model has a full information feedback, which yields a regret of order $\sqrt{T\ln N}$ against the best constant-spread strategy.
elements.sent-2411-028: In our setting, instead, we compete against a continuum of strategies parametrized by ask-bid pairs.
elements.sent-2411-029: This forces us to simultaneously control estimation and approximation error.
elements.sent-2411-030: Moreover, and crucially, our market model does not have a full information feedback, as our reward function depends on the taker's private valuations which remain unknown.
elements.sent-2411-031: Hence, we need to carefully exploit the structure of the reward function to compensate for the missing information.
elements.sent-2411-032: Please see \Cref{sec:additional for an extensive list of related works.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We study the question of market making with instant clearing in an online learning setup.
elements.sent-2411-034: Here, trading happens in discrete time steps and an unknown stochastic process governs the prices of the asset being traded as well as the private valuations assigned to the asset by market participants (see details in~\cref{sec:setting ).}
elements.sent-2411-035: The market maker is an online learning algorithm that posts a bid and an ask at the beginning of each time step and receives some feedback at the end of the time step.
elements.sent-2411-036: We are interested in controlling the regret suffered by this learner at the end of $T$ time steps.
elements.sent-2411-038: In both models, the learner can see the price (called the market price) at which they are able to offload their position at the end of the time step.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, in the realistic feedback model, they can only see whether their bid or the ask (or neither) got successfully traded, whereas in the full feedback model, they also get to see the private valuations of their counterpart in the trade.
elements.sent-2411-040: For each feedback model, we consider the i.i.d.\ setting, where the unknown stochastic process is i.i.d., as well as the adversarial setting, where no such assumption is made on the process.

elements.sent-2411-066: To understand the complexity of proving a lower bound, we now compare the trade-offs between regret and amount of feedback in hard instances of our setting and of other related settings.
elements.sent-2411-067: The three main ingredients in the standard lower bound approach for online learning with partial feedback are:.
elements.sent-2411-068: Hard instances, constructed as ``$\e$-perturbations'' of a base instance obtained by slightly altering the base distributions of some of the random variables drawn by the environment.
elements.sent-2411-069: The amount of information acquired when playing an action, quantified by the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the distribution of the feedback obtained when selecting the action in the perturbed instance and the corresponding feedback distribution when selecting the action in the base instance---the higher, the better.
elements.sent-2411-070: The regret of an action, simply measured by the expected instantaneous regret of the action in the perturbed instance---the smaller, the better.
elements.sent-2411-071: In hard instances of $K$-armed bandits, an arm is drawn uniformly at random from a $K$-sized set and assigned an expected reward $\e$-higher than the other arms in the set.
elements.sent-2411-072: Selecting an optimal arm costs zero regret and provides $\Theta(\e^2)$ bits of information.
elements.sent-2411-073: Selecting a non-optimal arm, instead, provides no information and costs $\e$ regret \cite{auer2002nonstochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-074: This implies that any algorithm has only one way to acquire information about an arm, i.e., to play that arm.
elements.sent-2411-075: A similar situation occurs in other settings, including those with continuous decision spaces.
